coredata.dc:title: Synergistic promotion of photoelectrochemical water splitting efficiency of TiO2 nanorods using metal-semiconducting nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Subramanian, Alagesan
Pan, Zhenghui
Li, Hongfei
Zhou, Lisha
Li, Wanfei
Qiu, Yongcai
Xu, Yijun
Hou, Yuan
Muzi, Chen
Zhang, Yuegang
coredata.dc:description: Highly efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting has been achieved by TiO2 nanorods (TNRs) decorated with Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The experimental analysis has indicated that the AuNPs has contributed to the plasmon resonance energy transfer/surface plasmon resonance-mediated hot electron injection and the GQDs contributed to the improved electron injection. The synergistic effect, which could be due to exciton-plasmon interactions and/or nonresonance energy transfer between the AuNPs and GQDs, is attributed to the superior PEC activity of the TNRs, which lead to a high photocurrent density of 1.75mAcm−2 at 1.23V vs RHE.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water splitting
Photoelectrochemical activity
TiO2 nanorods
Graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Synergistic effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: AuNPs and GQDs loading on TNRs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the as-prepared GQDs. Inset shows the size distribution of the GQDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs dispersed in water and photograph of the GQDs solution (Inset) taken under normal light (left) and UV light (right). (c) Down-conversion and (d) Up-conversion PL spectra of the GQDs, respectively.
SEM images of (a) 45° tilted cross-sectional view and (b) top view of TNRs grown on FTO substrate, respectively. (c) XRD patterns of the as-grown TNRs on FTO substrate.

(a) TEM image of AuNPs and GQDs-loaded TNRs sample. (b) XPS spectrum of the AuNPs and GQDs-loaded TNRs electrode. (c) Au4f XPS spectrum of the AuNPs. (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the four electrodes. Inset shows the enlarged portion of the light absorption property of the four electrodes in the visible region.

(a) Linear sweep voltammetry of the four photoanodes recorded at a scan rate of 20mVs−1. (b) Chronoamperometric J-t curves collected at 1. 23V vs RHE with a repeated on/off cycles of 10s. (c) IPCE spectra of the four photoelectrodes.
(a) Energy level diagram of the PEC system. (b) Schematic diagram showing the main mechanism of the enhanced PEC performance of the AuNP/GQD@TNR electrode.

head.ce:title: Synergistic promotion of photoelectrochemical water splitting efficiency of TiO2 nanorods using metal-semiconducting nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: i-Lab, Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionics, Chinese Academy of Science, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Suzhou Institute of Nano-Tech and Nano-Bionics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Testing and Analysis Center of Suzhou University, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Testing and Analysis Center of Suzhou University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Physics, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly efficient photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting has been achieved by TiO2 nanorods (TNRs) decorated with Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The experimental analysis has indicated that the AuNPs has contributed to the plasmon resonance energy transfer/surface plasmon resonance-mediated hot electron injection and the GQDs contributed to the improved electron injection. The synergistic effect, which could be due to exciton-plasmon interactions and/or nonresonance energy transfer between the AuNPs and GQDs, is attributed to the superior PEC activity of the TNRs, which lead to a high photocurrent density of 1.75mAcm−2 at 1.23V vs RHE.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting remains a promising technology to meet the human demand for energy by converting solar energy into chemical energy through the splitting of water for easy storage, transportation and utilization in the form of hydrogen [1–3]. For energy applications, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is one of the most widely studied photocatalysts and the nanostructured TiO2 is a very attractive candidate due to its large surface area, strong photocatalytic efficiency, high resistance to photocorrosion in aqueous solution, good chemical stability, nontoxicity, abundant and inexpensive [4–6]. However, existence of grain boundaries in the nanocrystalline TiO2 acts as carrier trapping sites [7]. Therefore, reducing the densities of grain boundaries and trap stats would improve the charge transportation with in the TiO2 nanostructure. Among various TiO2 morphologies, one-dimensional (1D) nanostructures such as nanorods [8], nanowires [9], and nanotubes [10] provide direct pathway for electron transfer and favor faster transportation of electronic charge carriers due to minimized grain boundaries and extended diffusion length [8,11]. Recently, the TiO2 nanorods (TNRs) with single crystalline rutile phase have attracted researchers’ attention as well because of their structure controllability and better electron transport property [12,13]. Previous studies have demonstrated that the rutile TNRs have promising PEC properties for hydrogen production by water splitting [14,15]. Rutile has two obvious advantages over anatase; superior chemical stability and slightly narrow energy bandgap. Furthermore, synthesis of single crystalline rutile TNRs on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass by hydrothermal processing technique is facile, low-cost and environmentally-benign [8,16].

However, there are still fundamental problems concerning the efficiency of photocatalytic activity of TiO2-based nanostructures, which include limited absorption of sun light and numerous electron-hole recombination [17]. Many strategies have been devoted to resolve these problems. Thus, various methodologies such as TiO2 band gap engineering by metals, nonmetals and transition metals [5,18–20], sensitization with dyes [21] or semiconductor quantum dots [22–25], formation of black and red titania [26,27], and crystal facet engineering [28] have been employed to improve photocatalytic performance. In recent years, gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been intensively explored and demonstrated to be promising materials for improved photocatalytic activity because they can strongly interact with light in the visible and infrared region through localized surface plasmon resonance (SPR) derived from the collective coherent oscillation of surface electrons and also due to their good photostability [29–32]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have also received much attention in the past few years due to its unique properties such as the quantum confinement and edge effects when their sizes are down to 10nm. These new physical properties can induce size-dependent bandgap, and unique optical and electronic properties, which make them excellent materials for photovoltaics devices, catalysis, fuel cells, drug delivery, bioimaging, biosensors, light emitting diodes and so on [33–36]. In the past few years, researchers have studied GQDs for their large optical absorptivity, tunable fluorescence, upconversion emission, hot electron injection, long term photostability, chemical inertness, nontoxic and low cost [34,35,37,38]. TiO2 nanoparticles (TNPs) and GQDs complex system enhanced the photocatalytic ability in the degradation of methylene blue [34]. The S, N co-doped GQDs and TNPs composites showed remarkable photocatalytic activity by degradation of rhodamine B compare to that of bare TNPs [39]. Recently, GQDs have been coupled with TiO2 nanotubes arrays (TNAs) for photocatalysis. The GQDs-sensitized TNAs exhibited high photocatalytic performance in the photo-degradation of methylene blue experiment [40]. The hydrogen evolution rate was remarkably improved by the combination of GQDs with CdS-modified TNAs [41]. However, the use of GQDs as photocatalysts in PEC cell for hydrogen generation is still limited and requires further research. The optical properties of the semiconductor and metal nanoparticles (SNPs-MNPs) are strongly modified comparing to single NPs. Therefore, the interaction between SNPs-MNPs is an interesting phenomenon, and could be utilized for light enhancement mechanism in the PEC devices. However, it is surprising that almost no work has been published in studying the PEC water splitting utilizing GQDs and AuNPs SNPs-MNPs combination.

In this paper, we have utilized the unique combination of AuNPs and GQDs, for the first time to the best of our knowledge, to effectively enhance PEC performance of TNRs. The significantly improved PEC water splitting efficiency is attributed to the extended light absorption due to the synergistic effect aroused between AuNPs and GQDs.

Graphite was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Shanghai. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and was used as-received. Deionized (DI) water (18.2MΩ) was used throughout all the experiments.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite powder by modified Hummer’s method [42]. An additional preoxidation procedure was performed prior to the GO preparation. The graphite powder (20g) was added to concentrated H2SO4 (30ml), K2S2O8 (10g) and P2O5 (10g) and heated at 80°C for 6h under stirring. The resultant mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. Then, the mixture was diluted with DI water (3.5l), filtered, and washed with DI water until the water reached neutral pH. The product was dried in a vacuum drier. The preoxidized graphite was used to prepare GO solution. In brief, 0.5g of preoxidized graphite was stirred in 12ml of H2SO4 followed by slow addition of 1.5g of KMnO4 in an ice-cold water bath (0°C). The mixture was then stirred at 35°C for 2h. Then, 24ml of DI water was added drop wise into the mixture. The reaction system was heated again at 80°C for 30min and cooled to room temperature. Then, 1.25ml of H2O2 was added after the drop wise addition of 70ml of DI water into the mixture. The resultant product was filtered through microporous membrane and washed with 125ml of 5wt% HCl aqueous solution in order to remove metal ions, and then washed with large amount of water using centrifugation method. The GO product was suspended in DI water and subjected to dialysis in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 8000Da) for several days to remove acids and metal ions completely. Then, the GO solution was untrasonicated for 30min to get homogeneous GO solution. Graphene sheets (GSs) were obtained from chemical reduction of GO. Briefly, 200μl of N2H4·H2O and 350μl of NH3·H2O were mixed with 100ml of 0.5mg/ml GO solution. The solution mixture was heated at 100°C for 3h and cooled to room temperature. The resultant solution was freezed and dried in vacuum drier to obtain GSs.

GQDs were synthesized using chemical reduction method with sodium borohydrate (NaBH4) [43]. In a typical synthesis of GQDs, 50mg of GSs were mixed with concentrated H2SO4 (10ml) and HNO3 (30ml). The mixture was heated and refluxed at 100°C for 24h to oxidize the GSs. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with 250ml of water, filtered through 0.22μm microporous membrane and washed with water to remove acids. Then, the filter cake was redispersed in 40ml of DI water. The pH of the solution was tuned to 8 with 1M NaOH aqueous solution. Then, 0.5g of NaBH4 was added slowly and stirred at room temperature for 3h. The suspension was heated upto 85°C for 16h (cutting and reduction step). The resulting black suspension was filtered using a 0.22μm microporous membrane to remove larger sized particles. The GQDs solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3000Da) for several days to remove the acids and salts.

TNRs were grown on FTO substrate by hydrothermal method [44]. In this procedure, 0.3ml titanium n-butoxide was added into 10ml of HCl and 10ml of H2O mixtures under magnetic stirring. The FTO substrates (16Ω/□, 2cm×1.5cm) were cleaned with acetone, isopropyl alcohol and distilled water prior to use. The FTO substrates were placed in the poly(tetrafluoroethelene) lined autoclave container (50ml volume) at an angle against the wall of the container with conducting surface facing down. The solution was transferred into the autoclave containing the FTO substrates. The hydrothermal treatment was conducted at 150°C for 6h. Finally, the substrates were washed thoroughly with distilled water and dried at ambient temperature. The as-grown TNRs on FTO were annealed at 450°C for 1h. A TiCl4 post-treatment was applied to the sintered electrodes by immersing the samples into a 70mM TiCl4 aqueous solution and kept in an oven at 70°C for 30min. After washed with water and drying, the electrodes were sintered again at 450°C for 1h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AuNPs and GQDs loading on TNRs
ce:section.ce:para: The AuNPs were deposited onto the TNRs (denoted as ‘AuNP@TNR’) through a facile ionic layer adsorption and thermal reduction approach [45]. An aqueous solution of 10mM HAuCl4·4H2O was prepared and pH of the solution was adjusted to 4.5 using 1M NaOH aqueous solution. The TNRs electrode was then immersed in the HAuCl4 solution for various time (1h, 2h, 3h, and 4h) to form AuCl(OH)− 3 complex on TNRs surface. The TNRs electrode was thoroughly washed with water and dried in air. Then, calcination was carried out at 300°C for 3h to grow metallic AuNPs on TNRs. GQDs were attached onto the TNRs (denoted as ‘GQD@TNR’) by a simple impregnation of the TNRs in the as-synthesized GQDs solution for 6h, 12h and 18h. The compositions of AuNP/GQD@TNR were obtained by immersing the AuNP@TNR electrode (immersed in the HAuCl4 solution for 3h) in the GQDs solution for 6h, 12h and 18h. Finally, the samples were washed with water and dried at 60°C in an oven. All the samples were characterized only for the optimized conditions, which gave best PEC performance.

The working electrodes were covered with silicone paste except the active area of 0.25cm2. All electrochemical measurements were performed with an electrochemical workstation (ZAHNER ZENNIUM) in a three electrode configuration using the as-prepared photoelectrodes as the working electrode, a Pt grid as counter electrode, a Ag/AgCl in saturated KCl as reference electrode and 1M NaOH aqueous solution as electrolyte (pH=13.6). The photocurrent density and voltage (J-V) curves (reported with respect to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale following the Nernst equation: ERHE =EAg/AgCl +0.1976V+0.059pH) were recorded under a standard AM 1.5 sunlight illumination (SCIENCETECH SS150 solar simulator, Canada). The light intensity was calibrated to be 100mW/cm2 using a standard Si solar cell. The time-dependent photoresponse curves were carried out by measuring the photocurrent under chopped light illumination (light/dark cycles of 10s) at a fixed bias of 1.23V vs RHE using the same instrument and setup. The statistic stability test for the AuNP/GQD@TNR electrode was measured under a standard AM 1.5 sunlight illumination at a fixed bias of 1.0V vs RHE using the same setup. The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) was also measured with the same testing system using ZAHNER CIMPS in the wavelength range of 300–800nm under 1.23V bias vs RHE.

Surface morphology and thickness of the TNRs film were measured by scanning electron microscope (SEM, HITACHI S-4800). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was used to take TEM-EDS mapping to analyze element distribution on the sample and to examine the GQD size using a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin TEM, operated at 200kV. Raman spectrum of the GQDs was collected on a Raman spectroscope (HORIBA Scientific) equipped with a CCD detector and confocal microscope using 532nm laser excitation. The photoluminescense properties of the GQDs were recorded with HITACHI F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer. The absorption spectra of the GQDs, AuNPs and TNRs-based samples were recorded using a UV/vis spectrometer (Lambda 750, PerkinElemer Precisely). The crystalline phases of the titania electrodes were analyzed from X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern obtained using BRUKER D8 ADVANCE diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation source at a scanning rate of 1°/min from 2θ=20° to 80°. The FEI Quanta 400 FEG SEM equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) were used to determine the elements present in the as-prepared samples.

Fig. 1 (a) shows TEM image of the GQDs and their size distribution. It is observed that the GQDs are well-dispersed, uniform and have narrow size distribution in the range of 2–10nm with an average diameter of around 6nm. The optical properties of the GQDs are shown in Fig. 1(b–d). Green fluorescence is observed when the GQDs are irradiated with 365nm UV light as shown in the inset of Fig. 1(b). It is clearly noticed from the absorption spectrum (Fig. 1(b)) that the as-prepared GQDs show a strong absorption in the UV region without any prominent absorption peak and extends its tail to red region, which could be due to different size distribution of GQDs. The absorption onset is at around 520nm and the bandgap of the GQDs is estimated to be ∼2.40eV. Fig. 1(c and d) show the corresponding emission spectra of the as-prepared GQDs. The as-prepared GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent down-conversion emission as well as up-converted emission. Interestingly, the PL spectrum shows broader peaks centered at ∼530nm. The excitation-dependent PL behavior is common phenomenon observed in carbon-based fluorescent materials. Different PL emission behavior of the GQDs is known to originate from different size, shape and defects [33,46]. The up-converted PL property could be possibly attributed to the multiphoton active process [38,47].

As illustrated in Fig. 2 (a and b), the SEM images show vertically grown, well-aligned, densely packed and uniformly distributed TNRs on FTO substrate. The length of the TNRs film is calculated to be ∼3.5μm and diameters of the TNRs are ranging from 50nm to 150nm with an average diameter of 90nm. XRD has been conducted to characterize the phase and crystallinity of the as-grown TNRs. From the XRD pattern (Fig. 2(c)), it is identified that the as-grown TNRs have pure tetragonal rutile phase. The significantly enhanced (002) diffraction peak indicates that the TNRs preferentially along [001] direction with growth axis perpendicular to the substrate as confirmed in SEM and are well crystallized [6,48].

TEM image of the AuNPs and GQDs loaded on TNRs is shown in Fig. 3 (a). From the TEM image, it is clearly seen that AuNPs and GQDs are attached onto the TNRs surface. The average particle size of the AuNPs is 8nm. Fig. 3(b) presents the XPS spectrum of the AuNP/GQD@TNR electrode. The binding energy of approximately 85eV, 284.5eV, 460eV and 530eV are assigned to Au 4f, C 1s, Ti 2p and O 1s, respectively, which confirm that the AuNPs and GQDs are attached onto the TNRs surface. The detailed representative XPS spectra of Au 4f and C 1s regions are displayed in Fig. 3(c) and Fig. S1 (SI). The peaks at ca. 83.6eV and 87.3eV in Fig. 3c are assigned to spin-orbit splitting of the Au 4f states (Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2) of the metallic Au(0) species [49,50]. The peaks in the XPS C1s spectrum (Fig. S1 in the SI) at 289eV and 284.5 belong to the sp2 carbon and hydroxyl groups present in the GQDs. The EDS elemental spectra, SEM-EDS elemental mapping and TEM-EDS elemental mapping analysis (Fig. S2, S3 and S4 in the SI) of the samples also reveal the contents of C and Au and confirms the attachment and uniform distribution of AuNPs and GQDs on the TNRs. The elemental analysis of the samples by EDS have showed that mass percentage of the AuNPs and GQDs in the AuNPs and GQDs modified TNRs are ∼1.2wt% and ∼1.0wt%, respectively, which is consistent with the results obtained from the XPS analysis. The light absorption property of the TNRs before and after AuNPs and GQDs loading has been measured and is given in Fig. 3(d). The optical band gap of the as-synthesized rutile TNRs is calculated to be about 3.0eV, which agree well with the reported band gap value of the rutile TiO2 in the literature [51]. It is noticed that loading the AuNPs and GQDs on the TNRs exhibits an enhanced absorption in the UV–vis spectrum, which is expected to result in enhanced PEC activity. AuNPs-decorated TNRs exhibit a broad band centered at around 560nm (Inset of Fig. 3(d)), which is a characteristic feature of the SPR effect aroused from AuNPs [52].

The PEC performance for different immersion time are given in Fig. S5 (SI). We have achieved the best PEC efficiency for 3h immersion time in HAuCl4·4H2O and 12h in the GQDs solution. The J-V characteristics of the optimized electrodes are shown in Fig. 4 (a). The dark scans from 0 to 1.5V vs RHE for all these samples have only exhibited a negligible current density at μAcm−2 scale. When illuminated by light, the TNR electrode reaches a photocurrent density of 0.44mAcm−2 at 1.23V vs RHE. The current densities of the AuNP@TNR and the GQD@TNR electrodes are 0.93 and 1.14mAcm−2 at 1.23V vs RHE, respectively, which are much higher than that of the bare TNR electrode, implying that efficient charge generation and separation happened at the interface by the attached GQDs and AuNPs alone. A superior PEC performance is observed for the AuNP/GQD@TNR electrode with a current density of 1.75mAcm−2 at 1.23V vs RHE, which represents almost a four-fold increase in the current density over the bare TNR electrode. The photocurrent transient behavior of these four electrodes is shown in Fig. 4(b) and the photocurrent densities are consistent with the values obtained from the linear sweep voltammetry. The chronoamperometric J-t curves show that all samples have good photo-switching performance with fast response time, which imply that photogenerated electrons are rapidly transported from AuNPs and GQDs to TNRs without significant recombination [53,54]. Fig. S6 (SI) shows that the AuNPs and GQDs loaded electrodes have stable photocurrent with increasing measurement time and thus confirm the photostability of the electrodes. Moreover, there is no obvious current decay found with increasing measurement time indicating that the GQDs and AuNPs are firmly attached to the TiO2.

To investigate the PEC performance of AuNPs and GQDs modified electrodes, IPCE measurement has been carried out. From Fig. 4(c) it is seen that the AuNP@TNR electrode shows a noticeable improvement with respect to TNR electrode. This enhancement is attributed to plasmon resonance energy transfer (PRET) and/or SPR-mediated hot electron transfer effect [2,45,55–57]. Illuminating the AuNPs with light can excite surface plasmons and produce SPR induced localized electric-field amplification. It has been demonstrated by Pu et al. [45] that the electric-field intensity has greatly increased in the visible region as well as a noticeable electric-field increase in the UV region. The PRET effect describes the interaction of TiO2 with the SPR-induced electric-field amplification in the UV region. The increased electric-field due to AuNPs in the UV region overlaps with the TiO2 absorption, since the TiO2 absorption is in the UV region, and energy transfer takes place from AuNPs to TiO2. This interaction promotes the photon absorption of TiO2 and result in enhanced optical absorption [2,45,56,57]. The SPR-mediated hot electron transfer occurs when the excited surface plasmons decay to hot electron-hole pairs with the hot electrons transiently occupying the surface plasmon state [2,55] as shown in energy level diagram of Fig. 5 (a). If the hot electrons have sufficient energy, then they can inject into CB of TiO2. The GQD@TNR shows a better IPCE improvement. To understand the phenomena, we have estimated the HOMO and LUMO orbital level of the as-synthesized GQDs from cyclic voltammetry (Fig. S7 in the SI) and are displayed in the energy level diagram of Fig. 5(a). Upon light irradiation, electrons are excited from valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB) of the GQDs. The CB of the GQDs is located above the CB of the TiO2, which energetically inject the electron from the GQDs to TiO2, as shown in Fig. 5(a), leading to a higher IPCE. However, when both the GQDs and AuNPs are attached on the TNRs, there is a tremendous enhancement in the IPCE (Fig. 4(c)). It is worth noting that the IPCE enhancement of AuNP/GQD@TNR is not merely the sum of the individual AuNP@TNR and GQD@TNR. It has been reported in the biosensing applications that when a semiconducting QDs and metal NPs are in close proximity under certain conditions unique phenomena of exciton-plasmon interactions (EPI), wherein exciton energy is shared and oscillates between plasmonic and excitonic system, and/or exciton energy transfer (EET) via nonradiative dipole–dipole interactions takes place [58–61]. Essentially, the efficient EPI needs the excitonic absorption of the semiconducting QDs has sufficient overlap with the plasmon absorption of the metal NPs. For strong EET, the spectral overlap between the emission spectra of the semiconducting QDs and absorption spectrum of metal NPs is crucial [59]. Shi et al.[62] has already demonstrated the occurrence of Förster resonance energy transfer between GQDs and AuNPs in their S. aureus (a kind of food poisoning bacterium) gene detection experiment. The PL and UV/Vis spectra have been analyzed to understand the synergistic enhancement mechanism (Fig. S8 in the SI). There is a good overlap between the absorption spectra of AuNPs and GQDs, as well as between the emission spectrum of GQDs and the absorption spectrum of AuNPs, which support the possibility of EPI/EET between AuNPs and GQDs [58,59]. Therefore, from the above analysis, the IPCE enhancement could be attributed to the synergistic effect aroused from the EPI and/or EET between GQDs and AuNPs. In addition to the optical enhancement, the GQDs and AuNPs can also act as co-catalysts, which reduce recombination loss through surface passivation, promote interfacial charge transfer process [63,64] and improve the photocatalytic property of the TNRs.

On the basis of the above analysis, we propose a mechanism to explain the superior PEC activity of the AuNP/GQD@TNR electrode, as shown in the schematic diagram of Fig. 5(b). The attached AuNPs contribute PRET/SPR effect and the attached GQDs contribute electron injection from CB of GQDs to CB of TiO2. Synergetic effects from GQDs-AuNPs hybrids played a key role in the enhancement of PEC activity. The catalytic property of the semiconducting and metal nanoparticles also benefit the PEC enhancement in addition to the increased light capturing ability by these materials.

In summary, we have designed AuNPs/GQDs-decorated TNRs photoelectrode. The AuNP/GQD@TNR photoelectrode has achieved a superior PEC performance due to an efficient utilization of solar light. The improved PEC performance is due to PRET/SPR-mediated hot electron injection effect induced by AuNPs and improved electron injection from CB of GQDs to CB of TiO2. We have found synergistic enhancement effects, which could be originated from the interactions between the excitons of GQDs and plasmons of AuNPs, significantly contributes to huge PEC activity enhancement. And also, the good catalytic property of the AuNPs and the GQDs contributes to the PEC enhancement.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports provided by Chinese Academy of Sciences Visiting Fellowship For Researchers From Developing Countries (GRAND No. 2014FFJB0006), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21433013, 51402345 and 21403287), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20140383), and Suzhou Science and Technology Development Program (SYG201532) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.05.190.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Composite photoanodes for photoelectrochemical solar water splitting
3D branched ZnO nanowire arrays decorated with plasmonic au nanoparticles for high-performance photoelectrochemical water splitting
Photoelectrochemical water splitting performance of vertically aligned hematite nanoflakes deposited on FTO by a hydrothermal method
Plasmonic Au nanoparticles on 8nm TiO2 nanotubes for enhanced photocatalytic water splitting

Densely aligned rutile TiO2 nanorod arrays with high surface area for efficient dye-sensitized solar cells
Photoelectrochemical properties of TiO2 nanowire arrays: a study of the dependence on length and atomic layer deposition coating
High efficiency dye-sensitized solar cells based on hierarchically structured nanotubes
Quantum dot solar cells. Tuning photoresponse through size and shape control of CdSe-TiO2 architecture
Rutile nanowire arrays: tunable surface densities, wettability and photochemistry
Substrate-free, large-scale, free-standing and two-side oriented single crystal TiO2 nanorod array films with photocatalytic properties
Surface treated TiO2 nanorod arrays for the improvement of water splitting
Photoelectrochemical water splitting using dense and aligned TiO2 nanorod arrays
Growth of oriented single-crystalline rutile TiO2 nanorods on transparent conducting substrates for dye-sensitized solar cells
Photocatalysis on TiO2 surfaces – principles, mechanisms, and selected results

Effects of single metal-ion doping on the visible-light photoreactivity of TiO2

Homogeneous photosensitization of complex TiO2 nanostructures for efficient solar energy conversion
Quantum-dot-sensitized nitrogen-doped ZnO for efficient photoelectrochemical water splitting
Metal-free efficient photocatalyst for stable visible water splitting via a two-electron pathway
Effective nonmetal incorporation in black titania with enhanced solar energy utilization
A red anatase TiO2 photocatalyst for solar energy conversion
Synergy of low-energy {101} and high-energy {001} TiO2 crystal facets for enhanced photocatalysis
Enhancement of photoelectrochemical response by Au modified in TiO2 nanorods
